abstract: The catalytic conversion of carbon dioxide into methanol represents a critical pathway for the utilization of captured emissions, yet conventional copper-based catalysts typically require high operating pressures to achieve significant yields. This study explores the performance of size-selected $\text{Cu}_4$ clusters supported on $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ thin films for the hydrogenation of $\text{CO}_2$ under near-atmospheric conditions. Using in situ grazing-incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy, we characterized the oxidation state of the clusters during the reaction, revealing that the $\text{Cu}_4$ species are highly active in their reduced state at low $\text{CO}_2$ partial pressures. Experimental results demonstrate that these subnanometer clusters exhibit exceptional turn-over rates, surpassing those of recently reported low-pressure catalysts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations support these observations, identifying a lower activation barrier for the conversion of $\text{CO}_2$ to $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ on $\text{Cu}_4$ compared to bulk copper surfaces or larger nanoparticles. This enhanced activity is attributed to the unique coordination environment and strong adsorption properties of the small clusters. Our findings suggest that atom-precisely engineered copper clusters offer a highly efficient route for recycling $\text{CO}_2$ into valuable chemical feedstocks at reduced energy costs.
